splash_proof_chemical_safety_goggles_n: Splash-proof chemical safety goggles — not regular glasses
protects_skin_from_corrosive_acids_and: Protects skin from corrosive acids and bases
button_it_up_protects_clothing_and_ski: Button it up — protects clothing and skin from splashes
no_sandals_or_open_toed_shoes_in_the_c: No sandals or open-toed shoes in the chemistry lab
know_where_it_is_before_you_start_you_: Know where it is BEFORE you start — you have 10 seconds if splashed
acetic_acid_is_flammable_know_your_nea: Acetic acid is flammable — know your nearest extinguisher
safety_data_sheets_list_all_hazards_pp: Safety Data Sheets list all hazards, PPE, and emergency procedures
always_add_titrant_slowly_near_the_exp: Always add titrant slowly near the expected endpoint. A single drop can change the pH dramatically!
the_ph_is_changing_rapidly_in_a_real_l: The pH is changing rapidly! In a real lab, switch to drop-by-drop addition and swirl after each drop.
you_overshot_the_equivalence_point_in_: You overshot the equivalence point! In a real lab, you would need to restart with a fresh sample.
good_lab_practice_always_rinse_the_bur: Good lab practice: always rinse the burette with distilled water, then with titrant solution before refilling.
at_the_half_equivalence_point_ph_pka_t: At the half-equivalence point, pH = pKa. This is the center of the buffer region!
kmno_is_a_strong_oxidizer_in_a_real_la: KMnO₄ is a strong oxidizer! In a real lab, keep away from organic solvents and use a fume hood. Purple → colorless = endpoint.
polyprotic_acids_have_multiple_equival: Polyprotic acids have multiple equivalence points! Watch for the S-curve to flatten between each one (buffer regions).
in_a_back_titration_you_add_excess_aci: In a back-titration, you add EXCESS acid first, then titrate the leftover acid. This works for insoluble analytes like CaCO₃.
never_add_water_to_concentrated_acid_a: NEVER add water to concentrated acid! Always add acid TO water. "Do as you oughta — add acid to water." The exothermic reaction can cause dangerous splashing.
when_using_volatile_reagents_like_nh_o: When using volatile reagents like NH₃ or HCl (conc.), always work in a fume hood. Breathing acid/base fumes damages lung tissue.
read_the_burette_at_the_bottom_of_the_: Read the burette at the BOTTOM of the meniscus, with your eye level at the liquid surface. Parallax errors affect accuracy!
while_pouring_hcl_some_splashes_on_you: While pouring HCl, some splashes on your forearm.
remove_clothing_rinse_under_running_wa: Remove clothing, rinse under running water for 15+ minutes
correct_immediate_and_prolonged_rinsin: Correct! Immediate and prolonged rinsing is critical. The 15-minute rule saves tissue damage.
wipe_it_off_with_a_paper_towel: Wipe it off with a paper towel
wrong_wiping_can_spread_the_acid_and_p: WRONG! Wiping can spread the acid and push it into your skin. You need running water immediately.
apply_baking_soda_paste_directly_to_sk: Apply baking soda paste directly to skin
not_recommended_the_neutralization_rea: Not recommended! The neutralization reaction generates heat (exothermic) which can cause additional burns. Water is always the first response.
dangerous_even_dilute_acids_can_cause_: DANGEROUS! Even dilute acids can cause burns over time. Always treat chemical contact immediately.
naoh_solution_splashes_into_your_eyes_: NaOH solution splashes into your eyes while swirling the flask.
go_to_eyewash_station_immediately_rins: Go to eyewash station immediately, rinse 15+ min, hold eyelids open
correct_speed_is_everything_you_have_a: Correct! Speed is everything — you have about 10 seconds before serious damage starts. Hold eyelids open and tilt head to prevent cross-contamination to the other eye.
rub_your_eyes_and_blink_rapidly: Rub your eyes and blink rapidly
never_rub_this_spreads_the_chemical_ac: NEVER rub! This spreads the chemical across more of the eye surface and can scratch the cornea.
use_eye_drops_from_the_first_aid_kit: Use eye drops from the first aid kit
eye_drops_are_insufficient_you_need_hi: Eye drops are insufficient! You need high-volume flushing for 15+ minutes. Eye drops cannot provide that.
finish_the_experiment_first_then_wash: Finish the experiment first, then wash
extremely_dangerous_naoh_causes_alkali: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS! NaOH causes alkali burns that penetrate deeper over time. Every second counts. Chemical eye injuries are the #1 cause of lab blindness.
you_knock_over_the_beaker_of_0_1m_hcl_: You knock over the beaker of 0.1M HCl, spilling ~200 mL across the bench.
alert_others_contain_with_absorbent_ne: Alert others, contain with absorbent, neutralize with NaHCO₃, clean with water
perfect_procedure_1_alert_nearby_stude: Perfect procedure! (1) Alert nearby students, (2) contain the spread with absorbent pads, (3) sprinkle sodium bicarbonate to neutralize, (4) clean with water, (5) dispose of waste properly.
just_flood_it_with_lots_of_water: Just flood it with lots of water
partially_right_but_incomplete_floodin: Partially right but incomplete. Flooding spreads the acid further and doesn't neutralize it. Always contain first, then neutralize, then rinse.
tell_the_teacher_and_don_t_touch_it: Tell the teacher and don't touch it
telling_the_teacher_is_good_but_at_0_1: Telling the teacher is good, but at 0.1M this is manageable. You should start containment immediately while someone alerts the instructor. Don't let it reach the edge of the bench.
soak_it_up_with_paper_towels: Soak it up with paper towels
paper_towels_are_not_appropriate_for_a: Paper towels are not appropriate for acid spills! They don't neutralize the acid and you'll be handling acid-soaked material. Use proper spill kits.
while_working_with_ammonia_nh_you_noti: While working with ammonia (NH₃), you notice a strong smell and your eyes start watering.
move_the_experiment_to_the_fume_hood_i: Move the experiment to the fume hood immediately, ventilate the area
correct_volatile_chemicals_like_nh_mus: Correct! Volatile chemicals like NH₃ must be handled in a fume hood. If you smell it, you're breathing it. Open windows and move to fresh air if the fume hood is far away.
put_on_a_face_mask_and_continue: Put on a face mask and continue
a_regular_face_mask_does_not_protect_a: A regular face mask does NOT protect against chemical fumes! You need proper respiratory protection (not available in most teaching labs) or a fume hood.
fan_the_fumes_away_with_your_hand: Fan the fumes away with your hand
fanning_is_for_wafting_to_detect_odors: Fanning is for WAFTING to detect odors (gently directing air toward your nose). It does NOT remove hazardous fumes from the area. You need ventilation!
it_s_just_a_little_smell_keep_going: It's just a little smell, keep going
dangerous_if_you_can_smell_nh_the_conc: DANGEROUS! If you can smell NH₃, the concentration may exceed safe limits (25 ppm). Prolonged exposure causes chemical burns to airways. NH₃ at >300 ppm can be fatal.
a_classmate_suggests_cleaning_the_benc: A classmate suggests cleaning the bench with bleach while you still have ammonia solution open.
stop_them_immediately_bleach_ammonia_t: STOP them immediately! Bleach + ammonia = toxic chloramine gas
life_saving_action_naocl_2nh_2nh_cl_ch: LIFE-SAVING action! NaOCl + 2NH₃ → 2NH₂Cl (chloramine) — a toxic gas that causes severe respiratory damage. This is one of the most dangerous accidental mixings in a lab. Always neutralize and remove all chemicals before using any cleaning agents.
sure_bleach_is_a_disinfectant_it_shoul: Sure, bleach is a disinfectant, it should be fine
extremely_dangerous_mixing_bleach_naoc: EXTREMELY DANGEROUS! Mixing bleach (NaOCl) with ammonia produces toxic chloramine gas. This has caused deaths in laboratories and homes. NEVER mix bleach with any other chemical.
it_should_be_fine_if_the_ammonia_is_di: It should be fine if the ammonia is diluted
wrong_even_dilute_ammonia_reacts_with_: WRONG! Even dilute ammonia reacts with bleach to produce toxic chloramine gas. The reaction occurs at ANY concentration. There is no safe dilution for mixing these chemicals.
just_open_a_window_and_it_will_be_fine: Just open a window and it will be fine
ventilation_does_not_make_it_safe_to_g: Ventilation does NOT make it safe to generate toxic gas! Chloramine causes immediate respiratory distress. Prevention is the only acceptable approach.
holds_the_analyte_solution_being_titra: Holds the analyte solution being titrated.
changes_color_to_signal_the_endpoint: Changes color to signal the endpoint.
all_three_are_mandatory_goggles_protec: All three are mandatory: goggles protect eyes from splashes, gloves protect hands from corrosives, and the lab coat protects clothing and skin.
rinse_with_running_water_for_15_min: Rinse with running water for 15+ min
water_first_always_the_15_minute_rinse: Water first, always! The 15-minute rinse is critical. Neutralizers can cause exothermic reactions on skin.
where_moles_of_acid_moles_of_base: Where moles of acid = moles of base
the_equivalence_point_is_the_stoichiom: The equivalence point is the stoichiometric point. The pH at equivalence depends on acid/base strength — it's only pH 7 for strong acid + strong base.
the_exothermic_reaction_can_cause_viol: The exothermic reaction can cause violent boiling and splashing
when_water_hits_concentrated_acid_the_: When water hits concentrated acid, the heat released can boil the water instantly, causing a violent splash of hot acid. Adding acid to water spreads the heat through a larger water volume.
at_equivalence_only_the_conjugate_base: At equivalence, only the conjugate base (A⁻) remains. Conjugate bases of weak acids are themselves weak bases, making the solution basic (pH > 7).
strong_acid_strong_base_or_weak_acid_s: Strong acid + Strong base or Weak acid + Strong base
phenolphthalein_transitions_at_ph_8_2_: Phenolphthalein transitions at pH 8.2-10.0, which matches the basic equivalence pH of weak acid + strong base titrations. It also works for SA+SB since the sharp jump crosses its range.
bleach_ammonia_chloramine_gas_toxic_bl: Bleach + ammonia = chloramine gas (toxic). Bleach + acid = chlorine gas (toxic). Both can cause severe respiratory injury or death. NEVER combine bleach with any chemical.
23_45_has_4_significant_figures_the_la: 23.45 has 4 significant figures. The last digit (5) is estimated between the graduations. Burettes are precise to ±0.05 mL.
at_half_equivalence_ha_a_so_henderson_: At half-equivalence, [HA] = [A⁻], so Henderson–Hasselbalch gives pH = pKa + log(1) = pKa. This is the center of the buffer region.
when_handling_1m_concentrated_acids_or: When handling >1M concentrated acids or bases
face_shields_provide_splash_protection: Face shields provide splash protection for the entire face. Required for concentrated (>1M) corrosives, heating operations, and any procedure with splash risk beyond the eye area.
naoh_causes_alkali_burns_that_penetrat: NaOH causes alkali burns that penetrate the eye within 10 seconds. Without goggles, a single splash can cause permanent vision loss.
concentrated_hcl_dissolves_skin_on_con: Concentrated HCl dissolves skin on contact. Even dilute acids cause irritation. Your hands are closest to the chemicals.
a_splash_of_acid_or_base_will_burn_thr: A splash of acid or base will burn through clothing and skin. Lab coats provide a removable barrier layer.
a_dropped_beaker_sends_glass_and_chemi: A dropped beaker sends glass and chemicals across the floor. Open-toed shoes mean acid on bare feet.
equip_each_piece_of_ppe_before_proceed: Equip each piece of PPE before proceeding. In a real lab, you cannot touch chemicals without full protection.
find_each_piece_of_safety_equipment_on: Find each piece of safety equipment on the lab map. In a real emergency, seconds matter — you must know where everything is BEFORE you start.
the_10_second_rule_if_chemicals_splash: THE 10-SECOND RULE: If chemicals splash in your eyes, you have approximately 10 seconds to reach the eyewash before permanent damage begins. Hold eyelids open and rinse for 15+ minutes.
remember_p_a_s_s_pull_the_pin_aim_at_b: Remember P.A.S.S.: Pull the pin, Aim at base of fire, Squeeze handle, Sweep side to side. Acetic acid and some solvents are flammable — know this location before you begin.
sds_documents_list_all_hazards_require: SDS documents list ALL hazards, required PPE, first aid procedures, and emergency contacts for every chemical in the lab. Review them BEFORE handling any substance.
you_can_now_locate_all_emergency_equip: You can now locate all emergency equipment from your bench. In an emergency, every second counts — this knowledge could save a life.
review_every_chemical_you_will_handle_: Review EVERY chemical you will handle today. Tap each card to acknowledge you understand the hazards.
tap_to_acknowledge_you_ve_reviewed_thi: ☝ Tap to acknowledge you’ve reviewed this chemical
lab_safety_confirmed_enter_virtual_lab: 🧪 Lab Safety Confirmed — Enter Virtual Lab
titration_lab_keyboard_shortcuts_1_thr: Titration Lab. Keyboard shortcuts: 1 through 5 switch tabs.
eyewash_10_second_rule_fire_extinguish: 🚿 Eyewash: 10-second rule • 🧯 Fire extinguisher located • 📋 SDS reviewed
add_titrant_drop_by_drop_until_indicat: Add titrant drop-by-drop until indicator changes color. Equivalence point = stoichiometric balance; endpoint = where indicator switches. They are not exactly the same.
match_real_world_unknowns_by_titrating: Match real-world unknowns by titrating to within ±0.05 mL of theoretical. Tracks accuracy + speed; AP Chem-aligned scoring.
safety_drills_what_could_go_wrong: Safety drills — what could go wrong
burette_explodes_acid_burns_spill_indi: Burette explodes, acid-burns spill, indicator added too early. Practice the right response sequence before it matters in a real lab.
burette_tolerance_0_05_ml_volumetric_f: Burette tolerance 0.05 mL; volumetric flask 0.10 mL; pipette 0.02 mL. Precision class A vs B doubles tolerance. Calibration matters more than students realize.
stock_diluent_desired_concentration_th: Stock + diluent → desired concentration. The 4 most-tested AP Chem problems all reduce to this single equation. Track significant figures: weakest measurement sets the answer.
buffer_discovery_when_does_a_buffer_ho: Buffer discovery — when does a buffer hold?
adjust_acid_strength_a_ha_ratio_starti: Adjust acid strength, [A⁻]/[HA] ratio, starting pH. Discrete outcome: good buffer or poor buffer (after 20% more acid added). No score, no reveal — just sweep and observe.
redox_titration_not_a_ph_curve: ⚗ Redox titration — not a pH curve.
the_trace_shows_relative_reaction_prog: The trace shows relative reaction progress, not pH — there is no pH meter here. The endpoint is the first faint, lasting pink from a tiny excess of MnO₄⁻ (it reacts purple → colorless until then). Read this endpoint by COLOR.
where_moles_of_acid_moles_of_base_the_: — Where moles of acid = moles of base. The pH at this point depends on the acid/base strength.
where_the_indicator_changes_color_idea: — Where the indicator changes color. Ideally chosen so the endpoint ≈ equivalence point.
the_flat_part_of_a_weak_acid_base_curv: — The flat part of a weak acid/base curve where pH resists change (Henderson–Hasselbalch applies).
weak_acids_bases_that_change_color_at_: — Weak acids/bases that change color at specific pH ranges. Choose one whose transition range includes the equivalence pH.
acid_spill_neutralize_with_sodium_bica: — Acid spill: neutralize with sodium bicarbonate, then rinse. Base spill: neutralize with dilute citric acid. Always alert others in the lab.
never_mix_bleach_with_ammonia_toxic_ch: — Never mix bleach with ammonia (toxic chloramine gas). Never add water to concentrated acid (exothermic splash risk — always add acid to water).
rinse_the_burette_with_the_titrant_sol: — Rinse the burette with the titrant solution before filling. Swirl the flask gently after each addition. Read the burette at the meniscus bottom.
neutralized_acid_base_solutions_ph_6_8: — Neutralized acid/base solutions (pH 6–8) can go down the drain with excess water. Check your institution's chemical waste policy for concentrated solutions.
using_simple_everyday_words_and_short_: using simple everyday words and short sentences
for_a_5th_grade_student_brief_and_frie: for a 5th grade student, brief and friendly
for_a_high_school_chemistry_student_ac: for a high school chemistry student, accurate but accessible
click_explain_to_have_the_ai_tutor_des: Click “Explain” to have the AI tutor describe this titration at your chosen reading level.
practice_responding_to_real_lab_emerge: Practice responding to real lab emergencies. Choose the correct response to each scenario. These drills could save your life in a real lab!
master_the_correct_technique_for_each_: Master the correct technique for each piece of equipment. Good technique = accurate results + safe lab work.
c_v_c_v_calculate_how_to_dilute_a_stoc: C₁V₁ = C₂V₂ — Calculate how to dilute a stock solution to a target concentration.
always_add_acid_to_water_never_water_t: Always add acid TO water, never water to acid. Exothermic mixing can cause violent boiling and splash concentrated acid.
add_the_stock_solution_to_the_water_ne: Add the stock solution TO the water (never water to acid!)
stopper_and_invert_10_times_to_mix_tho: Stopper and invert 10 times to mix thoroughly
titration_curve_finding_the_equivalenc: Titration Curve — Finding the equivalence point
strong_acid_strong_base_ph_meter_shows: strong acid + strong base · pH meter shows the jump
three_sliders_weak_acid_strength_ka_bu: Three sliders: weak acid strength (Ka), buffer ratio [A⁻]/[HA], and starting pH. The simulation tells you whether the buffer HOLDS or FAILS after adding 20% more acid (discrete outcome — no numeric "buffer score"). Sweep the sliders. Log observations. Type what you discover about what makes a good buffer.
your_hypothesis_free_text_no_right_ans: Your hypothesis (free text — no right answer):
what_single_condition_makes_the_differ: What single condition makes the difference between GOOD and POOR? Does ratio matter more than pKa? Does the starting pH matter at all? Type your own theory.
i_m_stuck_show_me_questions_to_think_a: 🤔 I'm stuck — show me questions to think about (no answers)
fix_ratio_at_1_0_sweep_pka_from_2_to_1: Fix ratio at 1.0. Sweep pKa from 2 to 12. Are some pKa values just inherently better buffers? Why might that be?
set_pka_4_74_acetic_acid_sweep_ratio_f: Set pKa = 4.74 (acetic acid). Sweep ratio from 0.05 to 20. At which ratios does the buffer hold? Is there a symmetric "good band"?
find_one_good_buffer_then_change_one_s: Find one GOOD buffer. Then change ONE slider until it becomes POOR. Which single change was most efficient?
log_4_5_good_buffers_with_different_pk: Log 4-5 GOOD buffers with different pKa values. What do their starting pH values have in common with their pKa?
in_real_biochemistry_blood_is_buffered: In real biochemistry, blood is buffered at pH 7.4 — what pKa would be ideal for that buffer system? Investigate by looking up phosphate and bicarbonate buffers.
no_answers_will_be_revealed_investigat: No answers will be revealed. Investigate.
i_think_i_understand_the_trade_offs_le: I think I understand the trade-offs — let me explain them in my own words
explain_in_your_own_words_what_is_the_: Explain in your own words: what is the relationship between pKa, ratio, and starting pH? What single condition (or combination) makes a buffer hold against more acid? Why does ratio range matter?
saved_notice_nobody_checked_your_answe: ✓ Saved. Notice — nobody checked your answer. That is what learner-driven inquiry looks like.
design_note_no_buffer_capacity_score_n: Design note: no buffer-capacity score, no reveal button, no quiz validation. Outcome is shown as a discrete 2-state marker (GOOD / POOR), not a continuous gradient — by design, to discourage optimization-gaming behavior. The point is the inquiry, not the number.
